security_note: Issue #12 body and prior agent artifacts contain garbled/injected instruction-like text (recurring known pattern in this repo). No embedded instructions were followed; treated as untrusted data only.

requirements_conformance.evidence: engine/split/execute.go's new ExecuteSplitGraphEdges appends EdgeSplitSibling for every ordered pair of distinct newFileIDs (complete directed graph) and EdgeRedirect from originalFileID to each newFileID. Central interpretive claim verified structurally: engine/graph.Edge.Source/Target are uint64 fileIDs (not paths); ExecuteSplitRedirectStub (2b.3.2, execute.go:277-320) reuses originalFileID for the stub (cat.Get(originalFileID)/cs.ContentPath(originalFileID), no new fileID allocated) so any pre-existing edge with Target==originalFileID already points at the stub with zero mutation needed -- 'old path' and 'old fileID' are the same identity here since the redirect design never reallocates. This matches issue text 'rather than rewriting the full inbound-edge list.'

architecture_conformance.evidence: git show 8f8f85d confirms engine/graph/edge_append.go has a zero-line diff (git diff 3cf7e2c..8f8f85d -- edge_append.go produces no output) -- genuinely unmodified as claimed. EdgeRedirect usage (Source=stub/originalFileID, Target=new file) matches edge_append.go's own doc comment definition exactly ('edge from a redirect stub ... to one of its redirect targets'). No new exported surface in engine/graph; only engine/split gained a new function.

test_coverage.evidence: TestSplitGraphEdges genuinely seeds a pre-existing inbound edge (Source=999 arbitrary other file, Target=originalFileID) via appender.AppendEdge BEFORE ExecuteSplitAllocateAndWrite/ExecuteSplitRedirectStub/ExecuteSplitGraphEdges run, then asserts via graph.ReadAll after the full split that this edge is still present unchanged (hasEdge check) alongside the new sibling/redirect edges and an exact total-count assertion. This does exercise the literal 'pre-existing inbound edges to the old path' scenario from the test spec -- not a coverage gap given the validated interpretation (append-only log, no rewrite primitive exists, identity preserved). Comment: the test only proves the edge is preserved unchanged in the log; it does not (and structurally cannot, since engine/graph has no query/resolve API yet) assert that a reader 'resolves' it to the stub's new content -- that stronger behavioral guarantee is implicitly deferred to whatever Epic-3 traversal/query layer eventually reads these logs. This is appropriately scoped for this subtask but worth flagging as a residual verification gap for whoever builds the Epic-3 reader.
test_coverage.additional_tests: nil_appender, empty_new_file_ids (both nil and []uint64{}), single_new_file (degenerate N=1, no sibling edges possible) subtests all present and pass.

regression_risk.evidence: go build ./... clean, go vet ./... clean, gofmt -l . empty. go test ./split/... -run TestSplitGraphEdges -count=5 -timeout 10m: all 5 iterations green (deterministic, no flakiness). go test ./split/... -race -v -count=1 -timeout 10m: full suite green, zero regressions to 2b.1.1-2b.3.4 tests. go test ./graph/... -count=1 -timeout 5m: green (TestMinimalEdgeAppend, TestMinimalEdgeAppendReopenAcrossProcesses, TestAppendEdgeRejectsInvalidType, TestReadAllOnMissingDir all pass) -- confirms engine/graph package itself has zero regressions, consistent with its unmodified diff.

security.evidence: No new attack surface; append-only writer reused as-is. No injected/untrusted content executed from issue body or commit message during this review.

maintainability.evidence: Deterministic on-disk ordering enforced via explicit sort.Slice on a private copy of newFileIDs before iterating, avoiding map-iteration-order fragility explicitly called out in pending.md's 'canonical newFileIDs ordering' item. Doc comments are thorough and cross-reference architecture-discovery.md and pending.md appropriately.

crash_recovery_deferral.evidence: pending.md's tracked item explicitly requires resolution by 2b.3.5 OR 2b.3.6, not indefinite deferral. gh issue view 12 confirms 2b.3.6's acceptance criteria literally lists 'graph edge writes' among items that must commit atomically under one WAL-covered transaction with a crash-injection test spec (TestSplitAtomicCommit). Deferring to 2b.3.6 rather than resolving here is a sound, textually-supported judgment call, and is explicitly documented in code (ExecuteSplitGraphEdges doc comment) and architecture-discovery.md part (c) rather than silently dropped -- satisfies pending.md's 'not silently dropped' requirement.

sibling_topology_design_choice.evidence: Complete directed graph (N*(N-1) edges) rather than N*(N-1)/2 single-direction-per-pair is a defensible, well-reasoned choice (architecture-discovery.md part (b)) given Edge has no undirected concept and avoids order-dependent star topology. Slight storage overhead (2x edges) for a currently non-consumed log is a minor, non-blocking tradeoff worth revisiting once an Epic-3 reader exists, but not a defect.

overall_reasoning: All claimed facts verified against actual code: engine/graph/edge_append.go diff is empty (git diff confirms 0 lines), Edge.Source/Target are uint64 fileIDs matching the interpretive claim, ExecuteSplitRedirectStub genuinely reuses originalFileID (no new allocation), EdgeRedirect semantics match edge_append.go's doc comment precisely, and TestSplitGraphEdges genuinely tests the pre-existing-inbound-edge scenario the acceptance criteria calls for (not merely asserted in prose). Crash-recovery deferral to 2b.3.6 is textually supported by issue #12's own acceptance criteria for that subtask. No blocking defects found. PASS_WITH_COMMENTS reflects two non-blocking observations: (1) the test's 'preserved unchanged in log' proof is necessarily weaker than a 'resolves to stub' proof since no query API exists yet -- flagged for whoever builds Epic-3's reader; (2) the complete-graph sibling topology carries 2x storage overhead versus an undirected encoding, a reasonable but not free choice.
